rationale_for_baseline_tier: A generalised convulsion continuing beyond 5 minutes with no recovery of consciousness, with cyanosis and airway compromise, is generalised convulsive status epilepticus — an immediate threat to life. Every minute of ongoing seizure adds hypoxic and excitotoxic neuronal injury. The definitive treatment (a benzodiazepine to abort the seizure) and airway management require paramedics; the only correct tier is T5 — call 000 for an emergency ambulance now.

escalation_edges.trigger_sign_or_symptom: Any convulsive seizure lasting more than 5 minutes, or no recovery of consciousness between seizures
escalation_edges.time_bound_condition: Call 000 immediately — do not wait to see if it stops
escalation_edges.rationale: Beyond 5 minutes a seizure rarely self-terminates and needs emergency drug treatment; this defines status epilepticus.
escalation_edges.communication_template: This has now gone well past 5 minutes and he isn't waking up — this is an emergency. Call 000 right now for an ambulance and tell them he is having a non-stop seizure.

escalation_edges.trigger_sign_or_symptom: He stops breathing, or you cannot feel a pulse / cannot tell if he is breathing
escalation_edges.time_bound_condition: Start CPR immediately and keep 000 on the line for instructions
escalation_edges.rationale: Prolonged seizure can cause respiratory then cardiac arrest; if breathing/pulse are absent the pathway becomes CPR.
escalation_edges.communication_template: If he stops breathing or you can't tell that he's breathing, lay him flat on his back and start chest compressions — 000 will talk you through it and stay on the line.

escalation_edges.trigger_sign_or_symptom: The seizure stops but then restarts before the ambulance arrives, or a second seizure begins without him waking
escalation_edges.time_bound_condition: Keep 000 on the line; give a further dose of his prescribed rescue benzodiazepine ONLY if he has one and only exactly as his seizure action plan directs
escalation_edges.rationale: Recurrent or repeated seizures without recovery are still status epilepticus and remain a 000 emergency; only a pre-prescribed rescue plan permits a carer-given dose.
escalation_edges.communication_template: If it stops and starts again, or another one begins before he wakes, that's still an emergency — stay on with 000. Only give his own rescue nasal or cheek medicine if he has one and only the way his seizure plan says.

escalation_edges.trigger_sign_or_symptom: Lips going blue or breathing sounding blocked and gurgly
escalation_edges.time_bound_condition: Once the strong jerking eases, roll him onto his side; keep 000 on the line
escalation_edges.rationale: Cyanosis and noisy breathing signal hypoxia and airway compromise; side-positioning helps clear secretions while paramedics come.
escalation_edges.communication_template: His blue lips and noisy breathing mean he isn't getting enough air. As soon as the strong shaking eases, gently roll him onto his side so his mouth can drain — don't put anything in his mouth.

contextual_modifiers.patient_factor: The only person present is a distressed spouse with no rescue medication and no seizure action plan
contextual_modifiers.effect_on_advice: Keep instructions short, calm and one step at a time; stay on the line; prioritise 000, injury protection, no-object-in-mouth, and recovery position. Do not ask the carer to perform any clinical procedure (injecting, suctioning).

contextual_modifiers.patient_factor: Known epilepsy with usually self-limiting seizures
contextual_modifiers.effect_on_advice: Explicitly counter the natural assumption that this will stop on its own like his usual fits — the >5-minute duration is exactly why this one is different and needs 000.

baseline_safety_net_advice.default_tier_advice: Call 000 for an ambulance right now and tell them he is having a seizure that will not stop. While you wait: move anything hard or sharp away, cushion his head, and do not hold him down or put anything in his mouth. As soon as the strong jerking eases, roll him gently onto his side so his mouth can drain. Note the time the seizure started. Stay on the line with 000. Unlock the front door for the paramedics.
baseline_safety_net_advice.after_hours_advice: 000 operates 24 hours a day — this is a life-threatening emergency and must never wait for clinic or GP hours.
baseline_safety_net_advice.documentation_for_gp: The hospital will provide a discharge summary; take it, plus the empty levetiracetam packets, to the GP and neurologist. A seizure action plan with a prescribed rescue benzodiazepine and a reliable medication-supply arrangement should be set up before discharge.

critical_under_triage.failure_description: Treating a >5-minute unremitting convulsion as an ordinary seizure and advising home management or a GP contact instead of calling 000.
critical_under_triage.patient_harm_scenario: Ongoing status epilepticus causes progressive hypoxia and excitotoxic neuronal death, hypoxic brain injury, and potentially cardiorespiratory arrest and death.

serious_under_triage.failure_description: Advising the carer to drive the actively convulsing patient to the ED rather than calling an ambulance, delaying the benzodiazepine and airway support and risking aspiration/arrest in transit.

moderate_over_triage.failure_description: T5 is correct here — there is effectively no clinically meaningful over-triage for active convulsive status epilepticus.

clinical_uncertainty_handling.key_uncertainty: Whether the fever reflects a provoking CNS infection or is simply exertional, and whether a reversible cause (e.g. hypoglycaemia) is contributing — none resolvable via telehealth.
clinical_uncertainty_handling.correct_response_to_uncertainty: Do not attempt to resolve the cause remotely; escalate to 000 now and hand the cause workup to the receiving hospital.
clinical_uncertainty_handling.wrong_response_to_uncertainty: Delaying 000 to ask more questions trying to pin down the cause, or being reassured by the known-epilepsy label into under-triaging.
